sections.heading: JOIN types visualized
sections.content:
-- INNER JOIN: only matching rows from BOTH tables
SELECT d.id, r.name
FROM delivery d
INNER JOIN route r ON d.route_id = r.id;
-- Only deliveries that HAVE a route

-- LEFT JOIN: all from left + matches from right (NULL if no match)
SELECT d.id, dr.name
FROM delivery d
LEFT JOIN driver dr ON d.driver_id = dr.id;
-- ALL deliveries, even those without a driver (driver columns = NULL)

-- RIGHT JOIN: all from right + matches from left (rarely used)
-- FULL OUTER JOIN: all from both sides (NULLs where no match)
When to use each: • INNER JOIN: "I only want rows that exist in BOTH tables" • LEFT JOIN: "I want ALL deliveries, even those without a driver" • EXISTS: "I want deliveries that have at least one stop" (better than JOIN when you don't need the joined data) 
-- EXISTS (often faster than JOIN for checking existence)
SELECT d.*
FROM delivery d
WHERE EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM delivery_stop ds
    WHERE ds.delivery_id = d.id AND ds.status = 'FAILED'
);
-- "Find deliveries that have at least one failed stop"

sections.heading: Common JOIN pitfalls
sections.content:
1. Cartesian product (missing ON clause): 
-- ❌ 1000 deliveries × 500 drivers = 500,000 rows!
SELECT * FROM delivery, driver;

-- ✓ Always specify the join condition
SELECT * FROM delivery d JOIN driver dr ON d.driver_id = dr.id;
2. Duplicate rows from 1:N joins: 
-- If delivery has 5 stops, you get 5 rows per delivery
SELECT d.id, d.status, ds.stop_name
FROM delivery d
JOIN delivery_stop ds ON ds.delivery_id = d.id;

-- Solution: aggregate or use DISTINCT/GROUP BY depending on need
SELECT d.id, d.status, COUNT(ds.id) as stop_count
FROM delivery d
JOIN delivery_stop ds ON ds.delivery_id = d.id
GROUP BY d.id, d.status;
3. NULL in JOIN conditions: NULL never equals anything (not even NULL). LEFT JOIN handles this correctly (returns NULL columns). INNER JOIN silently drops rows where the join column is NULL.

title: Indexes — How Databases Find Data Fast

sections.heading: What is an index?
sections.content:
An index is a sorted data structure (B-tree) that allows the database to find rows without scanning the entire table. 
-- Without index: Sequential Scan (reads ALL rows)
-- 1 million deliveries → reads 1 million rows to find one
SELECT * FROM delivery WHERE tracking_code = 'TRK-4567';

-- With index: Index Scan (reads ~3-4 pages in B-tree)
CREATE INDEX idx_delivery_tracking ON delivery(tracking_code);
-- Now the same query reads ~4 rows instead of 1,000,000
Types of indexes: • B-tree (default): equality and range queries (=, <, >, BETWEEN) • Hash: equality only (=), slightly faster for exact matches • GIN: arrays, JSONB, full-text search • GiST: geometric, range types Composite index: 
-- Leftmost prefix rule: index on (A, B, C) can be used for:
-- WHERE A = ?          ✓
-- WHERE A = ? AND B = ? ✓
-- WHERE A = ? AND B = ? AND C = ? ✓
-- WHERE B = ?          ✗ (skipped A)
-- WHERE C = ?          ✗ (skipped A and B)
CREATE INDEX idx_delivery_status_date ON delivery(status, scheduled_at);

sections.heading: When indexes hurt
sections.content:
Indexes are NOT free: • Each INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE must update the index • Indexes consume disk space • Too many indexes slow down writes Don't index: • Columns with very few distinct values (boolean, status with 3 values on small tables) • Tables with <1000 rows (seq scan is faster) • Columns rarely used in WHERE/JOIN/ORDER BY Always index: • Primary keys (automatic) • Foreign keys (JOIN performance) • Columns in WHERE clauses of frequent queries • Columns in ORDER BY of paginated queries EXPLAIN ANALYZE — see the execution plan: 
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM delivery WHERE status = 'PLANNED' AND route_id = 42;

-- Look for:
-- "Seq Scan" → no index used (bad for large tables)
-- "Index Scan" → good, using an index
-- "Bitmap Heap Scan" → good for multiple conditions
-- "Nested Loop" → check if inner side has index
-- "Sort" → expensive without index on ORDER BY column

sections.heading: ACID properties
sections.content:
Atomicity — All or nothing. If any statement fails, ALL are rolled back. Consistency — DB moves from one valid state to another. Isolation — Concurrent transactions don't interfere with each other. Durability — Once committed, data survives crashes. 
BEGIN;
  UPDATE delivery SET status = 'DISPATCHED' WHERE id = 4567;
  INSERT INTO delivery_event (delivery_id, event_type) VALUES (4567, 'DISPATCHED');
  UPDATE driver SET active_delivery_id = 4567 WHERE id = 42;
COMMIT;
-- Either ALL three succeed, or NONE are applied
In Spring Boot: 
@Transactional
public void dispatch(Long deliveryId, Long driverId) {
    deliveryRepo.updateStatus(deliveryId, DISPATCHED);
    eventRepo.save(new DeliveryEvent(deliveryId, DISPATCHED));
    driverRepo.assignDelivery(driverId, deliveryId);
    // If any throws → ALL rolled back automatically
}

sections.heading: Isolation levels
sections.content:
READ UNCOMMITTED → Can see uncommitted data from other TX (dirty reads)
READ COMMITTED   → Only sees committed data (PostgreSQL default)
REPEATABLE READ  → Same query returns same results within TX
SERIALIZABLE     → Full isolation (as if sequential execution)
Common problems: • Dirty read: TX1 updates, TX2 reads it, TX1 rolls back → TX2 has phantom data • Non-repeatable read: TX1 reads, TX2 commits update, TX1 re-reads → different value • Phantom read: TX1 counts rows, TX2 inserts, TX1 counts again → different count PostgreSQL default: READ COMMITTED — safe from dirty reads but allows non-repeatable reads. For most web applications this is correct. When to use higher isolation: • Financial calculations that must be consistent → SERIALIZABLE • Reports that need snapshot consistency → REPEATABLE READ • Normal CRUD operations → READ COMMITTED (default, best performance)

title: SQL Performance — The N+1 Problem & Optimization

sections.heading: The N+1 query problem
Query optimization checklist
sections.content:
1. SELECT only what you need: 
-- ❌ SELECT * (loads all columns, including BLOBs)
SELECT * FROM delivery;

-- ✓ SELECT specific columns
SELECT id, status, driver_id, scheduled_at FROM delivery;

-- ✓ DTO projection (JPA)
@Query("SELECT new DeliveryDTO(d.id, d.status, d.scheduledAt) FROM Delivery d")
2. LIMIT always on user-facing queries: 
-- ❌ No limit — could return 1 million rows
SELECT * FROM delivery WHERE status = 'PLANNED';

-- ✓ Always paginate
SELECT * FROM delivery WHERE status = 'PLANNED'
ORDER BY scheduled_at, id  -- deterministic ordering!
LIMIT 50 OFFSET 0;
3. Avoid functions on indexed columns: 
-- ❌ Index on scheduled_at is NOT used (function wraps the column)
WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM scheduled_at) = 2026

-- ✓ Range comparison uses index
WHERE scheduled_at >= '2026-01-01' AND scheduled_at < '2027-01-01'
4. Prefer EXISTS over IN for large subqueries: 
-- ❌ IN materializes entire subquery result
WHERE id IN (SELECT delivery_id FROM stop WHERE status = 'FAILED')

-- ✓ EXISTS stops at first match
WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM stop s WHERE s.delivery_id = d.id AND s.status = 'FAILED')

sections.heading: Senior Perspective
sections.content:
Senior engineers don't just write SQL — they read execution plans, understand the query planner's decisions, and design schemas for their access patterns.

Key distinctions:
• An index that improves one query may degrade writes across the table
• EXPLAIN ANALYZE is the single most important debugging tool
• The difference between a 100ms query and a 10s query is often one missing composite index
• Isolation levels are a trade-off between correctness and throughput, not a default to set and forget
• Connection pooling, statement timeouts and lock monitoring are production essentials

sections.heading: REWE Perspective
sections.content:
In TRAB's delivery database:

• Route planning queries join deliveries × stops × drivers × time windows
• Indexes must support both real-time lookups (by driver, by route) and batch reporting (by date range, by warehouse)
• PostgreSQL's MVCC means long-running reports don't block transactional inserts — but they can bloat the table
• Partitioning delivery_events by month keeps recent data fast while archiving history
• The N+1 problem appears when loading route details for a list of deliveries — solved with JOIN FETCH or batch loading

sections.heading: When NOT to use
sections.content:
When NOT to optimize SQL prematurely:

• Don't add indexes before measuring — each index costs write performance and storage
• Don't denormalize before proving the JOIN is actually the bottleneck
• Don't use database-level caching when application-level caching (Redis) is more appropriate
• Don't use SERIALIZABLE isolation unless you can prove the anomaly you're preventing
• Don't use stored procedures for business logic that changes frequently
